subheadline: AI systems for mission-critical work. Zerg works with teams whose products depend on hard technical systems: hardware, simulations, data and control planes, vendor integrations, migrations, and production workflows.
value_proposition: An autonomous AI operating loop that models real environments, generates and validates software against live signals, and continuously monitors for drift — purpose-built for mission-critical technical systems.
target_audience: Platform and infrastructure engineering teams working on hardware, vendor integrations, legacy migrations, data pipelines, and operational tooling where correctness is expensive and AI chat tools fall short.

metrics: 24 days for a large JavaScript to TypeScript migration with tests and reviewable checkpoints

pricing.price_anchoring: Value framed around team leverage and economics: 'Ship the work that used to be uneconomic' and 'a cadence that used to require a much larger team'

features: Operating loop: Map (model the real system), Build (generate/modify software), Prove (validate against live signals), Run (continuous monitoring and drift detection)
Connectors to email, files, calendars, ticketing, CRMs, warehouses, vendor APIs, cloud infrastructure, and custom hardware
CVE remediation with continuous detection, fix, and verification
Large-scale code migration and modernization with reviewable checkpoints
Agent memory, permissions, and recovery for safe access to live systems
differentiators: 'We do not stop at prompt-to-code' — full operating loop beyond code generation
Validates against live services, simulators, devices, and production signals — not just unit tests
Keeps watching after delivery so drift becomes a fix path, not a fire drill
Works against devices, simulators, bench rigs, logs, and signal chains instead of screenshots
Connectors treated as 'first-class work, not afterthoughts'
trust_signals: Specific, quantified proof points (24-day migration, 2-3 integrations/week)
Technical specificity signals domain depth (hardware-in-the-loop, signal chains, control planes)
Explicit acknowledgment of hard problems ('The hard part is not generating code once')

design_patterns.layout: Single long-scroll page with numbered section structure; hero followed by operating loop visualization, mission statements, runtime steps, connectors, proof metrics, and use cases
design_patterns.color_scheme: Dark/black background implied by technical tone and 'Live' indicator; likely monochromatic with accent color
design_patterns.typography: Clean, technical sans-serif with numbered sections and bold section headers
notable_patterns: Numbered step framework for operating loop (Map/Build/Prove/Run)
Metric callout blocks with large numbers
Live status indicator on operating loop diagram

emotional_tone: Confident and technical with a subtle urgency — positions itself as the serious tool for teams that have already been burned by simpler AI approaches
key_objections_addressed: 'The hard part is not generating code once' — directly preempts concern that AI just generates throwaway code
Addresses drift and maintenance: 'When requirements, APIs, or dependencies drift, Zerg detects the change and creates the follow-on fix'
Addresses safety of live system access: 'Agents need permissions, memory, and verification'
Addresses scope limitations: explicitly calls out hardware, simulators, and signal chains — not just web APIs
missing_or_weak: No customer logos or named references — zero social proof from recognizable companies
Pricing is completely absent — creates friction for evaluators
No demo video or interactive product preview
'Try Zerg Cloud' CTA has no visible destination or explanation of what cloud means in this context
No team/founder credibility signals (no About page link, no LinkedIn profiles)
Waitlist model with no indication of timeline or how to accelerate access
Navigation item 'Loop' duplicates content covered under 'Runtime' — slightly confusing IA
No FAQ section to handle common technical questions
standout_elements: Extremely tight, coherent positioning — 'mission-critical' is repeated and operationally defined, not just marketing language
The Map/Build/Prove/Run loop is a compelling and memorable framework that differentiates from generic AI coding tools
Honest framing of the hard problem ('The useful AI frontier is not another chat box') builds credibility with skeptical technical buyers
Use case specificity (CVE remediation, hardware-in-the-loop, JS→TS migration) signals genuine domain depth
The 'Bring us the system no one wants to own' closing CTA is unusually resonant and memorable for the target audience
